content:
[S1]: The Earth moves around the Sun in a big circle called an orbit.
[READER]: Hmm, I know the Earth moves, but what does orbit mean? I want to know why that makes seasons.

[S2]: The Earth is also tilted a little bit, like it’s leaning over while it goes around the Sun.
[READER]: Oh, tilted? Like when I lean to one side? That sounds interesting! I want to know how that makes it different.

[S3]: Because the Earth is tilted, different parts of Earth get more sunlight at different times of the year.
[READER]: More sunlight? So some places get more sun and some get less? That sounds like a clue!

[S4]: When your part of the Earth gets more sunlight, it feels warmer and we call that summer.
[READER]: Oh! So summer is when I get more sun. That makes sense. What about when it’s cold?

[S5]: When your part of the Earth gets less sunlight, it feels colder and we call that winter.
[READER]: Got it! Less sun means winter. So, it changes because of the sunlight. I want to know about spring and fall too!

[S6]: Between summer and winter, there are two times when the sunlight is almost the same, and we call those spring and fall.
[READER]: That sounds like a nice balance, like the Earth is not leaning too much. I wonder how long this all takes.

[S7]: It takes one whole year for the Earth to go all the way around the Sun and change through all the seasons.
[READER]: One year! That’s a long trip. So every year, the seasons come back again.

[S8]: So, the seasons happen because the Earth’s tilt changes how much sunlight different places get as it moves around the Sun.
[READER]: Wow, that’s cool! I never knew the tilt was so important. I think I understand seasons better now!
